headline: The Raw Numbers Mislead
subhead: Population-adjusted data reveals different fraud hotspots
key_insight: California leads in total reports because it's the largest state. Adjusted for population, DC, Nevada, Delaware, and Arizona have the highest fraud rates.

surprises.detail: District of Columbia: 1,054 reports per 100K vs California: 608 per 100K

surprises.finding: Arizona leads in loss per capita, not California
surprises.detail: Arizona: $4.63M per 100K population vs California: $4.28M per 100K

surprises.finding: Texas drops from #2 to #33 when adjusted for population
surprises.detail: Texas has a lot of fraud because Texas is big, not because Texas is especially targeted

surprises.finding: Small states punch above their weight
surprises.detail: Delaware (#4), Nevada (#2), and New Hampshire (#12) all rank higher per capita than their raw numbers suggest

interpretation.dc_theory: High per-capita rate may reflect higher reporting rates in urban, educated population rather than higher actual fraud rates. Alternatively, government employees and contractors may be targeted more frequently.
interpretation.sunbelt_pattern: Arizona, Nevada, Florida, and Hawaii all rank high in per-capita loss. Retiree populations with larger nest eggs may explain severity.
interpretation.texas_california_contrast: California ranks high on both raw and per-capita measures. Texas drops significantly, suggesting its raw numbers are primarily a population effect.
